comments.text: It seems this problem is currently just barely out of reach in the function field model. In $\mathbb F_q[T]$, at least for certain finite fields $\mathbb F_q$, there are "no large gaps between primes" results and uniform versions of the Hardy-Littlewood conjecture for $k=2$ with power savings. Combining these (along the lines of "Distribution of Large Gaps Between Primes by Funkhouser & Goldston & Ledoan), I believe one gets that the second moment of the number of primes in short intervals has the expected size up to the range where one can prove every short interval has a prime. This corresponds to a bound of the form $O ( N (\log N)^3)$. This might demonstate, in a different way, the difficulty of this problem.

The upper bound $O(N(\log{N})^3)$ can be easily obtained if we assume a weak form of Cramér's conjecture i.e. $d_n < C\log^2{p_n}$ for some constant $C$ and apply the Pólya–Szegő inequality that $(\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_k^2)(\sum_{k=1}^{n}y_k^2)\le \frac{1}{4}(\sqrt{\frac{m}{M}}+\sqrt{\frac{M}{m}})^2(\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_ky_k)^2$ where $m=m_xm_y$, $M=M_xM_y$, $m_x=\min\{x_1, x_2,\cdots, x_n\}$, $M_x=\max\{x_1, x_2,\cdots, x_n\}$, $m_y=\min\{y_1, y_2,\cdots, y_n\}$, $M_y=\max\{y_1, y_2,\cdots, y_n\}$.
But assuming that weak form of Cramer s conjecture, one actually has that p_n is bounded by 4C log^2(N) and so a O(N log^2(N)) bound is immediate.

Related to the difficulty of this problem, I have in the past used this as an example of a problem on this site 'at least as hard as the Riemann hypothesis'. This is, as far as I know, not precise, in that a positive solution to this does not imply the Riemann hypothesis, but given that e.g. it is a much stronger statement than every short interval $[X,X+O(X^{1/2+o(1)})]$ contains a prime, which currently is only known assuming RH, it feels like one would need to understand at least as much about primes as it would take to prove RH. I'm interested to hear from (human) experts whether they think this is reasonable, and perhaps even whether a precise implication that this would imply RH is known.

This description seems reasonable to me. I don't think there's any way it would imply RH, but any problem on the distribution of prime numbers not implied by RH and without even a vageuly-viable-seeming approach to prove it without RH has a reasonable claim to be harder than RH.

Note that a positive solution to this problem would automatically imply the prime gap bound $d_n \ll n^{1/2} \log n$, which was established by Cramer under the Riemann hypothesis but is not known in general; even the strongest forms of the prime tuples conjecture are not known to imply this bound. So it is extremely unlikely that one will be able to establish this result even assuming the prime tuples conjecture, if RH is not also assumed. (*Possibly* one might be able to make some progress assuming *both* RH and the prime tuples conjecture, but it is not clear how these rather different inputs could be combined.) EDIT: this advice given on the wiki is relevant to this discussion.

I have this proposed solution generated using ChatGPT 5.2 Pro: https://chatgpt.com/share/696c798b-1370-8005-8d14-fb8f9403c77f Here is the solution verification using Gemini 3 Pro and ensuring it is satisfied with the proof: https://gemini.google.com/share/e4c5b5c457a1 Solution summary: It converts $\sum d_n^2$ into a count of prime-free intervals and uses a standard sieve upper bound for prime-free intervals assuming a uniform Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple asymptotic. For rigor, I also prompted ChatGPT 5.2 Pro for a proof for the Selberg upper-bound sieve and corrected it with the feedback from Gemini 3 Pro. Disclosure: I'm not a mathematician by profession. Do you see a gap that I may have missed here?

I haven’t checked, but would be suspicious. The reason is the conversation is phrased like this is an *exercise with known solution*, and GPT-5.2 Pro is operating on the mindset that, even if its solution has gaps, it should be fixable (since it’s an *exercise*). This is a dangerous mindset for LLMs to be in. I’ll find time to check it later, though.

Hello, yes the part where it assumes the lemma was followed-up right after to be proven and it does it with some errors caught by Gemini. After feeding it the critique from Gemini, it improves the proof contingent on the Hardy-Littlewood k-tuple conjecture holding uniformly. I even pasted the full chat from ChatGPT to Gemini again and the evaluation remains consistent: https://gemini.google.com/share/df2deded69d3
I want to apologize to Patwa for confronting Gemini with a rude claim on his proof. I had not expected that such a full-mouth confrontation leads to a serious answer from the AI.

On page 87 (just above Theorem 3) of "Gaps between primes, and the pair correlation of zeros of the zeta-function" by Heath-Brown, he conjectures this same result but with the exact asymptotic constant $2$. Also, Theorem 8 here (see equation (9) on page 3 for the definition of $\mathcal{C}(x)$) in "Distribution of Large Gaps Between Primes by Funkhouser & Goldston & Ledoan almost derives that asymptotic conditional on some strong/uniform Hardy-Littlewood prime k-tuples conjectures. (The missing ingredient is no very large gaps).

You're right, thank you for coming with concrete data! My attempts were indeed futile and the LLM perfectly reconstructed past solutions probably learned from these papers. I will keep that in mind and the [AI disclaimer](https://github.com/teorth/erdosproblems/wiki/AI-contributions-to-Erd%C5%91s-problems) before I submit any solutions in the future.

While I think one should be *rather careful* posting AI solutions here, I also think it is useful to know that this problem follows from Hardy-Littlewood tuple (and prime gap?) conjectures. Other problems have also had similar comments.

After some back-and-forth with ChatGPT, it has come to believe that Hardy-Littlewood prime $k$-tuple conjecture was NOT shown to imply the problem either in Heath-Brown, Funkhouser-Goldston-Leodan, or Bhavya Patwa's GPT-5.2 Pro's shared chat. Feel free to scrutinize further, though! Note: what Boris said regarding "HL + large gap tail" is still correct from Theorem 8 in FGL. For Patwa: your shared chat does claim HL implies problem, which is still incorrect.

Cramer's bound was sharpened slightly to $\sum_{1 \leq n \leq N} d_n^2/n \ll \log^4 N$ (still assuming RH) in A. Selberg, On the normal density of primes in small intervals, and the difference between consecutive primes, Arch. Math. Naturvid. 47 (6), 87–105 (1943). Only off from the expected truth by one power of log. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Erdős also asks this question here (on page 440). Cramér's conditional proof of the bound $O(N (\log N)^4)$ is here at the end of the paper. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
